narration_segments.text: The feeling arrives before the thought, every single time. It starts in the solar plexus — that soft, unprotected space between the ribcage and the navel, the body's most vulnerable center, the place where armor cannot reach. A sinking. A hollowing. As though the center of gravity drops six inches and the body has to brace against falling into its own emptiness. Then the thought arrives, wearing the voice of authority: I am not enough. But the thought is secondary. The body felt it first. A contraction. A pulling inward. The shoulders round forward as though protecting the heart.

His name is Daniel. Daniel is forty-one. He runs a small woodworking shop in a town that does not need another woodworking shop, in a county that already has three, and he knows this, and he opens the doors every morning anyway because the smell of fresh-cut walnut and the weight of a hand plane are the two things in his life that have never lied to him. His hands are calloused from two decades of work. His joints ache in winter. His work is, by any honest measure, beautiful — the kind of furniture that stops people mid-sentence when they see it. Three months ago, a national design magazine featured one of his dining tables in a full-page photograph. The grain shone. The joinery was invisible. The caption called it one of the year's most significant pieces of contemporary craft. Daniel did not celebrate. He read the article twice, found two sentences he could interpret as faint praise rather than genuine admiration, and fixated on them for six days. He told his wife that the article was fine. Good exposure. Nice to be noticed.

These practices are designed to interrupt the self-contraction at the level of the body, where it lives, where it operates, where it exerts its authority. They do not require belief, faith, or philosophical commitment. They require willingness. The willingness to feel what is actually happening rather than what the mind says is happening. PRACTICE ONE: LOCATING THE CONTRACTION Sit. Close the eyes. Place one hand on the solar plexus and one hand on the chest. Breathe normally. Do not adjust the breath. Do not try to breathe deeply or calmly or mindfully. Breathe the way the body wants to breathe. Now, bring to mind one specific situation that activates the feeling of not being enough. Not the worst memory. Not the deepest wound.

The compressed teaching. Let it land in the center of the body, in the solar plexus, where the contraction lives and where the Bright waits. You are not broken. You are contracted. The contraction feels like identity — permanent, structural, definitional, the bedrock truth about a life — but it is an activity. It is something the organism does, moment by moment, breath by breath, like clenching a fist. And it can be observed. And what is observed — truly observed, steadily observed, without judgment and without agenda — loses its absolute authority. It becomes one event among many.
